text_clean: i selected this clamp for working with my mirrorless camera. there are times when clamping to a surface or a pole is more practical than setting up a tripod or monopod, so this kind of solution is useful in those cases. its a decent clamp system and it works well, though it does require some messing with it to get used to it if youve only worked with a tripod in the past.this idea is simple here. this is a clamp system that includes a couple of adapters for attaching cameras and accessories so that the camera can be held steady without having to set up a big, bulky tripod or having to hold the camera by hand. it comes with two clamps that can be connected via the ball joint adapter so that the system can convert to an anchor support for a pole as well not the intended use, but practical nonetheless.included in the kit is a 14 adapter that connects to a standard camera mount like my mirrorless, and an adapter for connecting a gopro. it can be used directly with a cell phone, but it is not ideal for that and does require being creative with the clamps not recommended better is using the clamp to hold a selfie stick which in turn holds the camera.the clamps hold tight as do the ball joints. it takes a little bit of fiddling but getting this set up exactly as desired is possible so that you can get the perfect angle. further, the system can hold a little weight and has no trouble at all holding my mirrorless camera steady.it seems to be made well and so far i have not had any issues with loose screws, stripped components, wobbly connectors when properly tightened or slipping grips. its still early on in working with this system but so far i see no red flags.i am happy with this clamp setup and i will get some good use out of it. its a practical solution when a tripod or other mount is not workable. its not particularly expensive, either.

text_clean: i have worked a lot with active noise canceling anc headphones and ear buds, and i was excited to try these as sony has really come on strong in recent years in this area. i usually do not wear ear buds, favoring fullsized overtheear can, but sometimes ear buds are the right answer. i am not unhappy with these.there are two aspects of these that need discussion the anc function and the sound quality. ill cover each of these separately.the anc function is good but is not topnotch. that is, its very effective at killing off ambient sound and it allows for listening to music at lower volumes without letting in surrounding sounds like fans and air handlers in an office or road noise in a car. it is not as aggressive as some of sonys other offerings, but still far and away better than cheaper headphones and ear buds available.i like the fact that i can touch one of the buds and the mode switches to allow more ambient noise through for conversation. this is a common feature for anc phones of any type anymore, and this pair of buds implements this well.the default sound profile is good but not perfect. the bass is good and not overdriven which is a refreshing change from most phones i have used. the midrange is a little off, with sound being a little muddy and with the slightest of distortion if youre looking for it. the highs are not sharp and also seem a bit muted like the midrange, though it is not severe enough to write these off by any means. i have worked with ear buds that have better balanced sound profiles, but i have also heard much worse. now, all that said, with sonys application its easy to access an equalizer to better balance the sound as desired. i have had no trouble tweaking the active profile in the equalizer to get these to sound better depending on the kind of music im listening to.these are sonys midrange buds, and are sold at a midrange price point. if these were priced like their topend buds i would be hesitant to recommend them. at the price point, though, theyre a good option and are better than most offbrand or cheap ear buds available.

text_clean: this is the first switch that i have owned that can be configured. thats a big plus. once you determine the ip address of the switch, you simply enter it in your browser, enter the default password which you can change and you are into the setup program that is internal to the switch. you can do individual settings for each of the 8 ports, if you know what the settings mean i didnt. this is an optional thing you can use the switch without any configuration just plug it in and it is ready to go. there are two reasons why i didnt give this a 5 star rating. 1 the indicator lights showing active ports are on the same side as the cables. since i want cables to go out the back, i cant see the lights with the switch facing me. my prior switch had lights on one side and cables went out the other. in all honesty, i cant say that the lights ever provided much benefit and could be distracting, so this might be an advantage to some people to have both lights and cables on the back. whats the point of the lights if you cant see them, unless you want the cables facing you? 2 in order to register the switch and get a program to determine the ip address of the switch so that you can configure it, you have to use a mobile device a smart phone and install the netgear insight application and set up a netgear account. strange that you need to use a device other than a computer to do this and it is rather a nuisance. apparently there is no windows version of netgear insight. you have to use your ios or android device. another problem i had is that you supposedly can change your password on your device, which i tried, but apparently the password did not meet the requirements, but was never informed of that by the program. i was able to change the password on the device after getting into it via the ip address. netgear makes good products and i have purchased other netgear products in the past with good results. this is another quality product, so if the placement of the lights doesnt bother you, i would recommend it, especially if you need individually configurable ports.

text_clean: this ihome bluetooth keyboard arrives already charged, which makes it a snap to set up. to use with an ipad, you just 1 switch the keyboard on, 2 press the connect button, 3 use ipad settingsgeneralbluetooth to find the keyboard, 4 click on the name of the found keyboard, and 5 enter the code that pops up. the keyboard requires ios 4.0 or later to connect to an iphone 3g or ipod touch.the featherweight keyboard is quite reasonably priced. its a little smaller than the keyboard on my toshiba netbook 3.5 rather than 4 high, 9.5 rather than 10 wide. because i have large hands, my fingers are somewhat scrunched up, but i was able to adapt quickly. the keys are flattopped, rather than contoured. there are the usual hot keys that wake the ipad, go to the home screen, adjust the volume, do fast forwardback, go to the search screen, etc. the keyboard goes into sleep mode automatically after 15 minutes of inactivity. a usb charger cable is included, so that the keyboard can be recharged by plugging it in to a computer or into an ac outlet if you have an adaptor.this keyboard does not attach to the ipad, a case, or anything else. i like having a freestanding keyboard, because i can place it anywhere thats comfortable for typing, well away from the ipad itself. the keyboard is molded so that it is angled slightly upwards when placed on a flat surface, and it has nice rubberized grippers that keep it from sliding around on my desktop. a black, velvetytextured, drawstring carrying bag is included to protect the keyboard in transit or in storage.quality control could be a problem with this keyboard. mine had a loose area at the lower right corner, which made the keys in that corner rattle. however the fix was easy pressing down firmly between the keys settled the corner under the clip that was there to hold it in place. i like this keyboard a lot, and will be using it regularly with my ipad. i rate it at 4.5 stars, rounded down to 4 stars because of the quality control issue.

text_clean: i selected this camera to mount on my front porch, with the intention of keeping a watchful eye on the front of the house. i was very skeptical of this camera because of the price and assumed that it would be a budgetgrade solution as a result. it has proven to be better than i anticipated.this is a singlecamera solution with no dedicated monitoring station. it connects via the wifi network and it can be monitored via phone which is handy when youre away. it includes a power supply and all of the mounting equipment minus the tools to set it all up for outdoor use.all of that sounds great, but as i write this the camera sells for under 40.it has not performed like a 40 camera. it has been rocksolid so far its stable, it has remained connected except for when i have had router issues, the image in the daytime is excellent and the nightvision mode is far and away better than i expected i can read license plates on cars in the driveway at night.this is not a complete security solution but rather is a single camera for zone security. there are more complete systems that offer more features and capabilities, but these cost a lot more and cover broader areas with multiple cameras. if you just need a single camera to monitor a fairly small area, and you have a wireless network to connect it to, this is a very affordable solution.

text_clean: i have had good success with kingston products in particular their ssd storage so i was pleased to have the opportunity to work with this encrypted thumb drive. it has a lot of promise and it works better than other encrypted drives i have used. there is one drawback, at least for me it does not support linux if it does, i havent figured out how yet.i have the 16gb version, and the thing is built like a tank. it has a metal casing around the inner workings and it can handle some abuse. thats good news if you keep these things in a briefcase or loose in a bookbag.setup is easy. when it is plugged into a windows machine i do not have access to a mac so i cannot speak to that use case, the software is available and can be used to set up the drive with a password. thats pretty straightforward. if you forget your password, you can wipe the drive and start over but you cannot recover your data. so, choose your password wisely.copy rates seem to be decent a little slower than similarsized drives i have that arent encrypted but not prohibitively so. its a tradeoff, and a small one at that.when i popped this drive into my linux machine, the kernel saw it immediately. however, all it could see was the setup partition which is approximately 100mb, a far cry from 16 gb. the drive is smart enough to hide its data from foreign osbased systems so your data is safe. linux is gaining users though, and being able to access the data from a linux system would be good, even if there was a tedious process to get to the partition but still requiring a password. this is better than other encrypted drives i have used in the past which made the data scrambled but was still accessible. however, not being able to use this drive under linux is a problem for me.to be fair, it is not advertised as compatible with linux.this is a good drive and from what testing i have done it works well. that said, it does not support linux and that is a negative for me. hopefully kingston will release a linuxbased version or a software layer for it in the future for now its only usable for me under windows.

text_clean: i like sandisk storage devices and have used a variety of their products with my electronic devices. i was intrigued by this one because of the small size relative to the capacity. the fact that it can talk to an android phone as well as a pc is an interesting feature. so far, i am pleased with it.i have the 128gb version and it is more than large enough for my needs, though there is a 256gb version for those that need more space. 20 years ago this kind of space would have been on a large hard drive and cost hundreds if not thousands. this one fits in my pocket and costs less than 20. times have changed.this device connects to my pc with a usb type a connector but at 3.0 speeds, meaning i can transfer plenty of data quickly without waiting half the day for it all to transfer. this is handy for moving picture archives or movie files which tend to be large. both my windows and my linux systems see this device and are able to communicate with it.the other interface to this device is a microusb connector, which works for older android phones. newer phones have a usbc connector so be sure that your phone can use this device if that is your intended purpose. it comes with an app for installing on your android phone to make it easier to manage. i dont have an android phone anymore but i do have a tablet that runs android, and this device connects to it without issue.it is small enough that it can fit in the coin pocket of my jeans with plenty of room to spare. this is both good and bad. on one hand, it is very easy to find room for this device no matter how burdened and heavily packed you are but it also means it is easy to misplace. id suggest adding a lanyard.so far i am pleased with this device. it is reliable, it has plenty of storage and it works with my pc as well as android phones. it would be more useful with a lanyard and a usbc option would be welcome, even if sold as a separate product entirely.

cot_summary: small enough to fit in your pocket, works with android phones, pc, and tablet

text_clean: i have a dell laptop that has served me well and i also have an older dell workstation that has been great. i had high hoped for this tower system, and unfortunately it doesnt quite meet expectations. if you are looking for an affordable gaming system, this one will work on the lower end.ill start with the positives.it comes with a 7thgeneration core i7 processor and it is definitely fast. it is not one of the low power consumption processors, so when you look at the cpu in the device manager you see 8 of them reported this is a good thing.everything is integrated with this system and there are plenty of ports. you dont need a separate graphics card though you can add one more on that in a minute, sound is built in, bluetooth is built in too and it even has integrated wireless networking.this is a very attractive system too, and its quiet. if you dont like a noisy system, this one has you covered.it comes with 1tb storage. its diskbased so its slower than an ssd but with that amount of space you have more than enough space for most uses.and, its affordable. it will not break the bank if you already have a monitor and keyboardmouse combo.and now for the bad news.i am not the least bit impressed with the graphics card. with modern games the more graphics memory you have the better, and having only 2gb video ram is not enough to get it done anymore. my son is an avid gamer and has demonstrated to me that his businessgrade laptop gets better frame rates. so, we are shopping for a better and more generous graphics card.another area where we will be expanding is the system ram. the ram is ddr4 and its fast, but the system comes with only 8gb. our present plan is to double it.no documentation? i know that the trend is to not include installrecovery media, but come on there should be more than just a quick start in the box.this is a decent desktop system that would be find in a business environment, but it falls a little flat when it comes to gaming. it needs a better graphics card well, at least more video ram and it needs more system ram to be a respectable gaming system.

text_clean: we have a tplink router, so getting an extender of the same brand seemed to fit the bill in extending our wifi issues. we live in a 150yearold apartment building with very thick walls. the cable for the tv and internet is located in the living room, whereas my office is on the completely opposite side of the building. there are about three walls in between, so my internet was shoddy at best. i have an iphone, macbook pro, a toshiba pc, and some handheld gaming consoles, and the only one to get decent service in my office was the mbp.i ordered the signal extender and stuck it into our bedroom, which is the halfway point from the router to my office. setting up the device was extremely easy, and took all of a couple minutes half of which was me making sure i did it right. at first, i noticed no boost in wifi to my office. before, the strength of signal reaching back there was about two or three bars on my toshiba pc, and the first or no arc on my iphone. however, after going about my business, i found that my toshiba pc only once has dropped under three bars, and sometimes even reaches its full signal strengthbut thats happened once. my iphone is about the same, but sometimes it does show full signal strength as well.i never get any more disconnected webpages in my office, and i can go about my work and nonwork without having to worry about taking the power off my pc and dashing to the living room.only once in the past month or so has it lost signal, but it picked up right away. it also acts as a nice nightlight with its warm blue glow, illuminating the path to the door so i can get out for a quick midnight getaway to the throne room and back.

text_clean: this is a 60 keyboard, meaning it doesnt have a dedicated numpad, arrow keys, or home keys. assuming you can live without those keys, read on.the first thing youll notice is the build quality. it feels really solid and wellmade. there is no flex in the keyboard, and it is made from series 5000 aircraft grade aluminum alloy whatever that means.the optipoint switches in the apex 9 mini were custommade by steelseries. they are optical switches, which means it uses light to detect when a key has been pressed optical switches have a quicker reaction time important for gaming than traditional mechanical switches.the optipoint switches are linear switches rated for 100 million keypresses. they dont require much force to attenuate, and the sound is pretty nice. steelseries says the switches are hotswappable meaning you can put your own switches into the keyboard, but the keyboard will only work with steelseries switches, not traditional mechanical switches. this is a big bummer.they keycaps are doubleshot pbt, which ensures the legend wont fade over time. additionally, it also allows for light to shine through, which i appreciate. steelseries also has software you can use to customize the backlight colors, etc. if you are into that.overall, if youre looking for a wellmade 60 keyboard, this is a good option! i dont love that the switches arent truly hotswappable, but otherwise, this is a good option!

text_clean: one of the problems i have seen with the latest crop of notebooks and ultrabooks that have come out is a lack of usb ports. theres usually a need for more ports than these systems have and it gets frustrating. a small usb hub like this is a good solution. that said, this particular hub does not have a separate power source so some consideration must be made when plugging in powerhungry devices.from a design stantpoint this is a solid unit, shaped to be accessible without being huge. it has a little weight to it and this is helpful for keeping it stable. the addition of velcro strips in the package to secure it in one place is a welcome inclusion and makes securing the hub very simple no matter what your configuration is.the hub provides four usb 3.1 type a ports. let me state what should be obvious this will not convert a usb 1.1 or 2.0 port into a 3.1 port, so plug it into a 3.1 port if you want the speed across the hub. for my specific uses, it does not need to be a highspeed hub and i am find with using it as a 2.0 hub, but admittedly thats slightly wasteful.there is no power port on this hub and there is no dc adapter in the box. so, any power that the hub needs comes through the onboard port into which it is plugged. thats fine for lowpower devices like mice, keyboards, memory sticks and so on, but if the intent is to plug in such things as usb hard drives with no external power supply this can cause some headaches. no, one drive isnt going to overload the hub, but if you have multiple drives attached there may be reliability issues when they starve one another for power. when the device plugged in has its own power supply like a big backup drive, this has little impact on the hub.the point is, plan carefully how you intend to use the hub.i like this hub and it does a good job for what it is. it has given me three extra ports on an ultrabook i use which is all i could ask for. the ability to power this hub with its own power supply would be the only area for improvement i could find.

baseline_summary: good hub for ultrabooks and notebooks, but needs to be considered for powerhungry devices
instruction_summary: good hub for ultrabooks and notebooks, but needs a power source with its own power supply
cot_summary: good hub for ultrabooks and notebooks, but needs to be considered for powerhungry devices
self_consistent_summary: good hub for ultrabooks and notebooks, but needs to be considered for powerhungry devices

text_clean: usb hubs are one of those computer extras that dont get a lot of attention or thought until one runs out of ports. when needed, though, a hub can greatly expand the usb capabilities of a system. this particular hub is one that comes with its own power supply, which is in my opinion better for most uses.this is an unassuming little device, about the size of a small harmonica and about as solid. its small size allows it to take up a small amount of desk space, tucking easily behind a monitor or case or, if being used with a laptop, behind the whole system. in my specific situation i have it plugged into a laptop with an external monitor attached to it so i have the hub just at the base of the external monitor where it is out of thew way but still easily accessible.in use, it works as it should. my one real test for the hub was to connect up an external usb hard drive, which does not work well with my other usb hub coincidentally, also a belkin product. when i attached the hard drive, the system immediately saw it and assigned a drive letter to it, just as if i connected it directly. this is how a hub should handle such a drive and i was pleased when there was no issue.the one complaint i have with this hub is that the power cord is not snug. when the hub is moved while attached it is very easy to disconnect it from the power supply. my workaround was to use a twist tie to attach the power cord to the usb cable that attaches the hub to the system, after which i can move the hub without any difficulty.this is a decent usb hub that works as it is supposed to work. other than the lessthansecure plug for power i have no complaints.

text_clean: after countless times of the ps4 controllers dying on me due to short battery life, media use on the ps4 was beginning to be a bit of an annoyance. thankfully, the pdp universal ps4 remote has come to the rescue well, for the most part. first and foremost, let me say that for the price, it does everything it needs to do and does it well. all of the buttons feel solid and are easy to use, the construction is good, the back is nice and grippy, it is lightweight and because it is a universal remote, it also works on your tv and other electronics. when attempting to use it with my tv it quickly synced itself without issue however i should note i have a sony television.if you are looking for a basic remote for bluray movies and netflix, this is it. it completely solved the battery issue i had with the ps4 remotes, as it has not died on me once. if you are a hulu user however, you can stop reading, it simply isnt good enough for hulu as it can behave somewhat erratically and will not go out of hulu menus once in them. outside of hulu however i have not had a problem with the controller and it functions great with anything else i may need.there are some minor issues with the remote, for example in order to change the volume you have to switch to the tv controls and then switch back to the ps4 to work with that. it may have just been my controller, and it was easily worked around use both controllers but some may find that an issue. when price is factored in, i would say that for the most part, the small annoyances are just that and most users will be able to overlook them. if i had to give a star rating it would be a 4 out of 5 star product. half a star off for lack of hulu compatibility and another half off for the little kinks it still has, overall a good product.

text_clean: i have wanted a backdrop for photography for a while and chose this one based on the brand name. i have some experience with powerextra products, in particular their replacement batteries for a digital camera i own. based on that previous experience i thought this would be a decent frame for a backdrop. its mostly good and i do like it but i found an issue with the clips.this is a fairly simple frame two tripods with cross braces on which to hang a backdrop. it assembles fairly quickly and also tears down just as quickly. i especially like that the whole things collapses down into a small package that is roughly the same size as a fishing rod carrier and is very portable. in a pinch the whole assembly can fit wholesale inside a duffel bag with other things too, though that somewhat defeats the purpose.this is not a heavyduty rig which should be obvious from the price. its a setup that i would say is ideal for travel as its relatively lightweight and easy to store, but sturdy enough to work in fairly static environments.the main issue i have with this setup is the set of four clips that come with it. i opened one of them and one of the tips on the clamping end popped off and went flying. it was easy enough to replace it, and it happened again on another one of the clips. i realized two things about the clips three of the four clips were assembled incorrectly which makes this much more likely to happen, and the tips can be lost fairly easily. the clips are the most easily replaceable part of the whole assembly so in the grand scheme of things this is not a showstopper but more thought could have been put into the clamp design and assembly without increasing costs appreciably.this is not a perfect rid for setting up backdrops but it works well enough and the cost is very reasonable. pay attention to the clip assembly, though, and ensure that the tips are installed correctly.

text_clean: capacitive styluses are not all alike. besides color, there are subtle differences in sensitivity, length, barrel size, weight, and attachment clips or lanyards. this targus stylus, which is 4.75 long exclusive of the capacitive tip is quite a good one, in terms of sensitivity. screens slide with minimal pressure, and selections register with only a light touch or tap.compared to other styluses that i tested, the targus stylus is the longest, has the widest barrel, and is the heaviest. the weight makes it tiring for me to use, but some individuals men, perhaps? may prefer a heftier, more substantial stylus like this one. because of the wider barrel, the tip is also wider, which makes it slightly less accurate for selecting small targets e.g., links displayed in web browsers in tiny fonts. the extra length is a plus, if you hold a stylus like a pointer away from the tip, rather than like a pencil close to the tip.this stylus has both a pocket clip and a metal attachment loop for attaching to a lanyard or keychain. i purchased mine at a local office supplies store. besides this stylus, i tested the asinb005o2ures minisuit universal capacitive stylus pen, the asinb0008g3id2 boxwave capacitive kindle fire stylus, and the asinb004gcjezu amazonbasics stylus for touchscreen devices. my favorite is the amazonbasics stylus, because of its 4.25 length, slim barrel, light weight, and excellent sensitivity. i rate this stylus at 4 stars i like it rather than 5 stars i love it, because i like the amazonbasics stylus slightly better.in case youre wondering, as i was, what a capacitive stylus is, it is a human interface device e.g., a mouse that uses capacitive sensing to measure changes of position on a touchscreen. it works just like a human finger on a touchscreen device, and you can often get more accurate navigation using a stylus. wikipedia has good articles on capacitive sensing and capacitive devices.

text_clean: i purchased my first flip camcorder about a year and a half ago when i was planning my trip to ireland. i bought a 60 minute basic flip and was pleasantly surprised how easy it was to use and the great quality of the video. the only downside i saw with the original was the fact that it was only 60 minutes. enter the new flip ultrahd camcorder which has a 120 minute capability. when i first received it, i couldnt wait to try it out. the quality of the video is beautiful and the hd capability really makes a huge difference in my pictures. i love the fact that the camera is smaller than my original camera and that i know can film up to 120 minutes. when i took the camera to ireland, i found that a 60 minute recording just couldnt be used for a days worth of sightseeing. i found myself taking short snippets, afraid that i would use up the entire capability until i was able to get back to the hotel and offload the recording. the short vignettes really came across choppy and i was yearning for a larger capacity. this 120 minute model, besides having better resolution, allows me the ability to take the camera with me full day to record. i havent seen the problem with battery life yet as i have seen others comment on, so i cant say anything negative. i just love my new flip and i highly recommend it for any one planning to take a vacation this year. it fits neatly into my pocket and has the capacity and quality that i am looking for!

text_clean: i am usually not a big fan of onear headphones. i have learned through experience that philips makes good headphones, though, and based on the brand name i selected this set of headphones to try out. so far i am pleased with them.as mentioned, these are onear phones. for me, i prefer overtheear phones which are more comfortable most of the time, and that is probably the only complaint i have with these. after wearing these for a while i need to take them off for a short time before i can put them back on and keep going. this is true with most onear phones i have tried. for onear phones, though, these arent bad and the ear fatigue is less than many i have used.this particular set boasts an ondemand bass boost, and for me thats a huge plus. most headphones have the bass boost baked right in and theres no way to turn it off. i dont care for heavyhanded bass gives me headaches, so for me it is a feature i will leave turned off most of the time. however, there are times when the bass needs some help, and these deliver. when activated, there is a very noticeable increase in bass response that will certainly make some people happy.so, about the sound profile... a set of headphones isnt worth any amount of money if they sound bad. these are fairly balanced phones when the bass booster is turned off. the highs are bright and clear, the midrange is warm and the bass is present but not overwhelming. the bass becomes front and center when the boost is turned on.these connect via bluetooth and they pair and establish connection quickly. i have no complaints.these are good onear phones that work well. theyre reasonably comfortable, and they connect well. the most interesting feature, to me, is the ondemand bass booster which is better than having a continuous bass boost thats built in. for me, thats a major positive point and is the reason i will continue using these. these sound good.
